title: Forgiving others part 3
scripture_text: ‘’Jesus said ‘For if you forgive other people when they sin against you, your heavenly Father will also forgive you. But if you do not forgive others their sins, your Father will not forgive your sins.’’
devotional:
We need to develop a disposition towards forgiveness.

One of the difficult issues regarding others is the question of their repentance, if they are not sorry, then how can we forgive?

We need to stand on the porch.

What do I mean? We all know and love the story of the ‘prodigal son.’ while telling this story Jesus said these words, ‘“But while he was still a long way off, his father saw him and was filled with compassion for him; he ran to his son, threw his arms around him and kissed him.’

The son was ‘still a long way off, and his father saw him and filled with compassion he ran.’ The father had developed a heart that longed to forgive his son, he longed for the opportunity to show mercy. Maybe he sat on his porch, scanning the horizon, looking and longing, ready to run at the least glimmer of repentance?

In some ways the question of the offending persons repentance is irrelevant, for this reason, the repentance of the ‘offender, although essential, will not increase my ability to forgive. It may increase my opportunity to forgive, but not my ability to forgive. If I am not living with the hope and expectation of forgiving the person who has hurt me before they repent, I am unlikely to feel different if they repent.

We forgive because we refuse to define the person who has hurt us by the harm they have done to us.

There are two pitfalls we need to avoid that will hinder us in our desire and ability to forgive. We can cause the hurt caused to us to become defining. So we develop a victim mindset. Sometimes, the pain is so deep, and the circumstances in which we were hurt were so dark that it is a huge battle not to live as a victim.

On the other hand, if we have been deeply hurt by someone, it is very difficult to see past what they have done, and their offence against us becomes the only way we choose to identify them.

When God forgives us he is choosing not to allow our sin to become means by which he identifies us. Does that mean he ignores or belittles our sin? Of course not. As sinful people we are more than just sinful people. And as forgiven people we have an opportunity to see people as more than those who have hurt us.

One final question:- What does it mean when Jesus said ‘But if you do not forgive others their trespasses, neither will your father forgive you.’

What is he saying? We know from other scriptures that nothing will separate us from the love of God, we also know that salvation is a gift from God, it is not something you can earn or deserve.. so how are we to understand these words? I think what Jesus may be saying here is, ‘If we choose not to forgive, God will hand us over to the consequences and the fruit of our unforgiving attitude.’

An unforgiving attitude will produce a rotten, bitter harvest in OUR lives, not in the lives of others. It will diminish us into small, graceless people who will use their energy to carry and maintain their resentment and bitterness.

When God hands us over to the consequences of being unforgiving, what happens?

Could I suggest the following?

1. Our fellowship with the Father is affected

2. The Holy Spirit is grieved.

3. Our prayers will be hindered.

4. The devil gains a foothold through our bitterness.

5. We waste time nursing a wounded spirit.

6. We become enslaved to the people we won’t forgive.

8. We become like the people we refuse to forgive.

9. We can develop physical and psychological problems.

Although the bible is insistent that we live as forgiving people, it never down plays how difficult and costly forgiving others can be. No one knows this truth more than God. The price and pain he paid to forgive us cannot be measured. When God the Father allowed his dear son to be murdered at the cross, one of the things we learn is that although the price of forgiveness is high, Love chooses to pay it.
prayer: Father may I have ‘a sitting on the porch’ attitude scanning for an opportunity to forgive.
html:
Forgiving others part 3

Study Text: Matthew 6:14-15
‘’Jesus said ‘For if you forgive other people when they sin against you, your heavenly Father will also forgive you. But if you do not forgive others their sins, your Father will not forgive your sins.’’

We need to develop a disposition towards forgiveness.

One of the difficult issues regarding others is the question of their repentance, if they are not sorry, then how can we forgive?

We need to stand on the porch.

What do I mean? We all know and love the story of the ‘prodigal son.’ while telling this story Jesus said these words, ‘“But while he was still a long way off, his father saw him and was filled with compassion for him; he ran to his son, threw his arms around him and kissed him.’

The son was ‘still a long way off, and his father saw him and filled with compassion he ran.’ The father had developed a heart that longed to forgive his son, he longed for the opportunity to show mercy. Maybe he sat on his porch, scanning the horizon, looking and longing, ready to run at the least glimmer of repentance?

In some ways the question of the offending persons repentance is irrelevant, for this reason, the repentance of the ‘offender, although essential, will not increase my ability to forgive. It may increase my opportunity to forgive, but not my ability to forgive. If I am not living with the hope and expectation of forgiving the person who has hurt me before they repent, I am unlikely to feel different if they repent.

We forgive because we refuse to define the person who has hurt us by the harm they have done to us.

There are two pitfalls we need to avoid that will hinder us in our desire and ability to forgive. We can cause the hurt caused to us to become defining. So we develop a victim mindset. Sometimes, the pain is so deep, and the circumstances in which we were hurt were so dark that it is a huge battle not to live as a victim.

On the other hand, if we have been deeply hurt by someone, it is very difficult to see past what they have done, and their offence against us becomes the only way we choose to identify them.

When God forgives us he is choosing not to allow our sin to become means by which he identifies us. Does that mean he ignores or belittles our sin? Of course not. As sinful people we are more than just sinful people. And as forgiven people we have an opportunity to see people as more than those who have hurt us.

One final question:- What does it mean when Jesus said ‘But if you do not forgive others their trespasses, neither will your father forgive you.’

What is he saying? We know from other scriptures that nothing will separate us from the love of God, we also know that salvation is a gift from God, it is not something you can earn or deserve.. so how are we to understand these words? I think what Jesus may be saying here is, ‘If we choose not to forgive, God will hand us over to the consequences and the fruit of our unforgiving attitude.’

An unforgiving attitude will produce a rotten, bitter harvest in OUR lives, not in the lives of others. It will diminish us into small, graceless people who will use their energy to carry and maintain their resentment and bitterness.

When God hands us over to the consequences of being unforgiving, what happens?

Could I suggest the following?

1. Our fellowship with the Father is affected

2. The Holy Spirit is grieved.

3. Our prayers will be hindered.

4. The devil gains a foothold through our bitterness.

5. We waste time nursing a wounded spirit.

6. We become enslaved to the people we won’t forgive.

8. We become like the people we refuse to forgive.

9. We can develop physical and psychological problems.

Although the bible is insistent that we live as forgiving people, it never down plays how difficult and costly forgiving others can be. No one knows this truth more than God. The price and pain he paid to forgive us cannot be measured. When God the Father allowed his dear son to be murdered at the cross, one of the things we learn is that although the price of forgiveness is high, Love chooses to pay it.

Prayer: Father may I have ‘a sitting on the porch’ attitude scanning for an opportunity to forgive.
Bible in a year reading plan: - Psalms 20-22
